advantages: Exists for every matrix (rectangular, singular, ill-conditioned)—no diagonalizability requirement.
Reveals rank, principal directions and energy spectrum via interpretable singular values.
Truncated SVD gives optimal low-rank approximations with explicit error bounds (Eckart–Young).
Numerically stable basis for least-squares, pseudoinverse and many regularized learning problems.
disadvantages: Full dense SVD costs O(min(mn², m²n))—expensive for very large matrices without structure or randomization.
Low-rank assumption fails when data truly needs high rank; truncation loses fine detail or rare patterns.
Interpretability of latent factors is not automatic—singular directions may not align with human concepts without post-processing.
Storage of full U and V can be large; in production, only thin factors or randomized sketches may be practical.
